questions.specification: Which of the following characteristics is most essential to an effective UCD team?

options.content: Market analysts with high-level data access

questions.specification: A health-tech startup is developing a smart wearable wristband aimed at elderly users. The device includes emergency alert features, real-time heart rate monitoring, fall detection, and GPS tracking. To ensure the product meets the needs of this specific target user group, the company applies user-centred design principles throughout the development process. See **Figures 1**, **2**, and **3**.

parts.content:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/b300dfc0-ff7d-46dd-84c5-5082d8bfd5e0)
Identify two key user needs that designers must consider when developing smart wearable technology for elderly users.
parts.markscheme:
- Large, readable screen/interface (vision accessibility) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Easy-to-use controls or voice activation (motor skill limitations) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline two advantages of using observation as a research method in the early stages of the product development process.
parts.markscheme:
- Provides insight into real user behavior rather than self-reported data <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps identify unarticulated user needs or pain points <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The design team created elderly personas as part of their UCD process, as shown in Figure 2.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/26c5ea0a-0f7a-48b7-aacb-7454741d91d0)

Explain how using a primary persona can help guide the design of the wristband in Figure 1.
parts.markscheme:
- Helps define key traits, goals and limitations of the intended user <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enables empathy-driven decision making in the design process <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Guides feature prioritization based on typical user lifestyle and concerns (e.g. fall detection, battery life) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The design team also established focus group discussions with elderly participants to aid their design processes.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/62ee79d5-8906-480c-be19-af4e0220da3f)

Explain how the team in Figure 3 can ensure that feedback from a focus group is reliable and representative of the wider user population.
parts.markscheme:
- Include a diverse sample across age, gender, mobility level, and tech literacy <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use neutral, open-ended questions to avoid bias <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Conduct multiple focus group sessions to triangulate findings <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Record and transcribe responses to accurately reflect data <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the potential benefits and limitations of involving users at all five stages of the UCD process (using methods such as those shown in **Figures 2** and **3**).
parts.markscheme:
- Increases likelihood that final design meets real user needs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Early identification of usability issues reduces costly redesigns <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourages user trust and product adoption <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Time-consuming and costly to engage users throughout every stage <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Conflicting user feedback may create design ambiguity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Users may lack the technical vocabulary to articulate feedback effectively <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Define the term “user-centred design” (UCD) and state one reason why it is important in product development.
parts.markscheme:
- **Define** user-centred design (UCD) as a design approach that focuses on the needs, wants, and limitations of end users at every stage of the design process <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** a valid reason for its importance, such as leading to products that are more usable, accessible, and effective for the target audience <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award [1] for a basic definition and [1] for a valid reason for importance.</note>

questions.specification: An inclusive design team is developing a self-service airport check-in kiosk to improve accessibility for passengers with diverse needs. The team uses user-centred design (UCD) principles to ensure the kiosk is intuitive, accessible, and efficient. They conduct observations in airports, build user personas, and test prototypes with different user groups.

parts.content:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/299d3609-4049-41d1-9412-5a92e22e9de3)

Identify two stakeholders, other than the end user, who would be involved in the development of the check-in kiosk shown in **Figure 1**.
parts.markscheme:
Airport staff (e.g. airline check-in desk employees) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Software/hardware developers <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for each relevant stakeholder up to [2 max].*

parts.content: Outline two limitations of using only online surveys to gather user feedback for this product.
parts.markscheme:
May exclude non-tech-savvy users (e.g. older travellers) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Limited depth of feedback compared to interviews or focus groups <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for each clear limitation up to [2 max].*

parts.content:
The design team wants to improve accessibility for passengers with various needs, and has a vision for an adjustable check-in kiosk.
 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/1efdfef1-a3c0-4b71-b2f9-c70fbaf0928b)

Explain how the team can apply empathy when designing for the user in **Figure 2**.
parts.markscheme:
Understand physical limitations (e.g. difficulty reaching screen) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Adjust screen height or provide angle flexibility for accessibility <marks>1 mark</marks>

Incorporate alternative input methods (e.g. voice or buttons at seated level) <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for each point demonstrating empathetic design insight up to [3 max].*

parts.content:
To deliver a product for those with accessibility needs, the design team creates a user journey map as seen in **Figure 3**. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/463065fa-f82c-4b15-9bf4-e6454ea7180d)
Explain how the user journey map shown in Figure 3 helps designers improve the kiosk experience.
parts.markscheme:
Identifies user pain points at each stage of the interaction <marks>1 mark</marks>

Helps visualize moments of confusion or delay <marks>1 mark</marks>

Allows prioritization of design improvements based on user frustration <marks>1 mark</marks>

Provides clear insight into emotional state and cognitive load at each stage <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for each well-explained point up to [4 max].*

parts.content: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of using multidisciplinary teams in the user-centred design of the airport kiosk.
parts.markscheme:
Advantages:

Brings varied expertise (e.g. engineering, psychology, UX design) for well-rounded solutions <marks>1 mark</marks>

Encourages broader perspectives on user challenges <marks>1 mark</marks>

Reduces the risk of design bias or oversight <marks>1 mark</marks>

Disadvantages:

Conflicting viewpoints can slow decision-making <marks>1 mark</marks>

Communication challenges between different disciplines <marks>1 mark</marks>

Requires more coordination and resources <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for each clearly developed advantage or disadvantage up to [6 max], with at least one of each.*

parts.content: Using an example, explain how scenario-building can help designers create a better solution for users with temporary disabilities (e.g. broken arm).
parts.markscheme:
Scenario: User with broken arm struggles to tap screen or hold documents <marks>1 mark</marks>

Helps identify features like one-handed operation or voice commands <marks>1 mark</marks>

Leads to more inclusive design that benefits all users <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for each valid point using a realistic scenario up to [3 max].*

questions.specification: Which of the following best describes a user-centred research method?

options.content: Field observation of people using similar products
Finite element analysis of mechanical structures
Cost analysis of existing production methods

questions.specification:
What does this image of a designer interviewing a user in their home represent in the UCD process?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXc4I8BHAHqMttgPpSR8RWpC560ug-RRwCpcF9K8mk_Yq4c3xsbLYAUR2nfSjXNQsj8JTDSTMA01_pBt8-XpXDZBK7rcXjdIvDqOJ1-Rl804KJTIAWkL5xrmsn3W5HR9Q8Uu5YnqAg?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: Creating a user persona from existing research
options.markscheme: This is a synthesis activity (persona creation), not the primary research activity shown (an in-home user interview).
An in-home interview is a user-centred research method used to gather insights about user needs, context, and behaviours.

options.content: Iterative prototyping and refinement of a concept
options.markscheme: Prototyping involves making and evaluating models or mock-ups; the image shows user research (interviewing), not prototyping.

options.content: Conducting usability testing of an existing prototype
options.markscheme: Usability testing typically involves users interacting with a product/prototype while being observed; the image shows an interview in the user's home rather than task-based testing.

questions.specification:
Transport for London (TfL) is designing digital interfaces for bus stops after research showed commuters want real-time arrival information, struggle with route planning, and need accessibility features. Research must understand diverse commuter needs including tourists, elderly users, people with disabilities, and non-native speakers.

![A mockup of a digital interface at a bus stop showing real-time arrival information for London buses, including route numbers, destinations, and estimated arrival times.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/aa4ac4a5-2ae1-4339-92d5-3e3b038ed5f2)

parts.content: State what is meant by accessibility research and give one example of an accessibility consideration relevant to digital interfaces at bus stops.
parts.markscheme:
- Accessibility research refers to research that focuses on understanding the needs, limitations, and barriers faced by users with disabilities or reduced abilities when interacting with a product or system. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- An example of an accessibility consideration is providing high-contrast text or audio announcements so users with visual impairments can access real-time bus information. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe how intercept surveys at bus stops could provide insights into commuter information needs and behaviours.
parts.markscheme:
- Explaining intercept surveys as short surveys conducted in the usage context (at the bus stop). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explaining what this captures (immediate needs, fresh pain points, current journey purpose). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifying a benefit (diverse sample, quick volume, low cost). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifying a limitation (time pressure, short responses, sampling bias). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mentions accessibility accommodations (large print, translations, interviewer assistance). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mentions combining with observation to validate responses. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Note: Award <marks>1 mark</marks> per point up to a maximum of <marks>4 marks</marks>.*

parts.content: Analyse how scenario-based research could help understand how different user groups interact with public transit information systems.
parts.markscheme:
- Scenario-based research places users in realistic travel situations (e.g. commuting, visiting, or navigating unfamiliar routes) to observe how they access transit information. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Using realistic scenarios reveals differences in behaviour between user groups such as tourists, daily commuters, or elderly users. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Scenarios help identify specific pain points at different stages of a journey, such as planning routes, finding platforms, or checking delays. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Observing users in context highlights accessibility barriers that may not appear in abstract questioning, such as time pressure or environmental noise. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Scenario-based research allows designers to compare how the same system performs across multiple user needs and abilities. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Insights from scenarios support more inclusive design decisions by ensuring interfaces work effectively in real-world transit situations. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the importance of inclusive research practices when designing for diverse urban populations.
parts.markscheme:
Indicative points to cover (answers may include):
- Representative sampling prevents excluding groups that rely on public transport most.
- Identifies barriers early (font size, language, cognitive load, physical reach).
- Improves equity and reduces harm in public services.
- Risk of designing only for "average" users.
- Methods: accessible materials, inclusive recruitment, assisted participation, translation, disability organisation partnerships.
- Mentions compliance and standards (accessibility guidelines) as one driver, without making it the only reason. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mentions co-design with disability groups to validate decisions. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Marking guidance:**
- <marks>1–2</marks>: General statements about inclusion with limited relevance to research practice.
- <marks>3–4</marks>: Identifies some reasons inclusion matters and a few practices, but limited depth.
- <marks>5–6</marks>: Balanced evaluation with clear links to research validity and service outcomes, includes practical inclusive methods.
- <marks>7–8</marks>: Strong evaluation showing how inclusive practices improve data quality, reduce bias, and produce safer, more usable public interfaces, with justified recommendations.

questions.specification: Which technique is **LEAST** relevant during user-centred research?

What does the following reflect in the context of user-centred design?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXdcE3oDixVc0ZXMM0miUSOanvLR0Du6hnCEysxgmo-XIpPsRBn0YmfwAqXBooGcQHiN5fS782DDjf2cX8Zr7JKxMJ6sN7d9p3Swb-O5SaSxWvJUt1u23H8Jcy0gtKwzv3Jc-q15Sg?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: Understanding the user’s tasks and experience

questions.specification: A UCD team notices their medical device is not intuitive for elderly users. Which action aligns best with the principles of UCD?

options.content: Reducing features to improve performance scores
Surveying young adult users for faster feedback
Conducting usability testing with elderly participants

questions.specification:
What is the main purpose of the design tool shown in the image?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/545127c3-081a-41bd-a8bb-8cf4bd41339b.jpeg)

options.markscheme: Technical schematics are created using CAD software or drafting tools, not persona templates.
The image shows a persona. Personas are fictional characters created to represent the different user types within a targeted demographic, focusing on their motivations, goals, and limitations.
Physical prototypes are built using workshop tools, 3D printing, or rapid prototyping techniques.
Engineering tolerances are part of detailed technical specifications and manufacturing drawings.

parts.content: Describe the role of multidisciplinary teams in user-centred design.
parts.markscheme:
- Multidisciplinary teams bring together expertise from various fields such as engineering, psychology, and marketing <marks>1</marks>
- Each discipline contributes a unique perspective to understanding the user, the product, and the context <marks>1</marks>
- Collaboration between specialists improves the accuracy of user research tools like personas and design specifications <marks>1</marks>
- These diverse viewpoints lead to a more holistic and inclusive final design that meets a wider range of user needs <marks>1</marks>

*Award [1] per valid point with development, up to [4 max].*

questions.specification: In the early stages of design, the use of personas allows the team to:

options.content: simulate user needs and behaviours for targeted design
eliminate the need for physical prototyping

questions.specification:
What stage of the UCD process does this best represent?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXeaflocFeOX--NQYj6t2sgRhOLFozU4nAxw6isGoGShxQ2wzAomG1qbdsjcj4QmUpKLSG03Yvv9Fuue3f1b14zv3sddA2KSldaZTES5dUM3nGFUN_YwmM4wM6NuIZmQNmXRQbGp?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

parts.content: Explain how surveys and focus groups can be used to gather user feedback in the design process.
parts.markscheme:
- **Collect** large amounts of quantitative data from a wide audience quickly using surveys <marks>1</marks>

- **Evaluate** user preferences, satisfaction, and usability through standardized survey questions to identify trends <marks>1</marks>

- **Gather** qualitative insights by using focus groups to explore user attitudes, motivations, and feelings in depth <marks>1</marks>

- **Facilitate** real-time discussion and clarification of ideas in focus groups to probe deeper into user responses <marks>1</marks>

- **Utilize** data from both methods to guide design iterations and make informed design decisions throughout the process <marks>1</marks>

- **Encourage** user involvement in the design process to foster a sense of ownership and ensure the product meets user needs <marks>1</marks>

6 marks total

<note>Award [1] per valid explanation or example, up to [6 max].</note>

questions.specification: User-centred design

parts.content: Describe the use of personas in the user-centred design process.
parts.markscheme:
- Define personas as fictional characters that represent the different user types within a targeted demographic <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that personas are developed using real qualitative and quantitative data gathered from user research <marks>A1</marks>
- Describe how personas help designers focus on specific user needs and preferences rather than an abstract 'average' user <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify how personas improve empathy during the design process, allowing for more user-focused decision-making <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain their role in facilitating clear communication between different members of the design and development team <marks>A1</marks>
- Describe how personas are used to test and validate design solutions against realistic user scenarios and use cases <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

<note>Award [1] per clear and relevant point, up to [6 max].</note>

questions.specification:
What does this activity best represent in the user-centred design process?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXdemOjqTfx--SykVHdhEUDJT_9qfD22Ui0kGbOJmWqohMRG6mU5Asdgm8GSCgi1VrYAXAn2vpWYdet-ltkEd_sKD1UOdlaFLecKTso6SyxrQyN6WKU3SyfntWLHw5T7yeeUBREe?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

What is this design activity an example of in user-centred design (UCD)?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXcQa4dlwgwxEgHw1TjMGPYdrsNKbfTQPzQM1PjJ4qiXjI_eeHS7RqQ_l0xFKGiB3cCz8Uo_C0tY-Rb_Wey4Uyu2zzZclm6q4mp4-H-7UGNoQ8myD2ALg6IE0cybucaUvNkvMx-X?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: Gathering needs and limitations of end users

parts.content: Evaluate the use of population stereotypes and demographics in guiding product development.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition/Explanation of Population Stereotypes**:
- Population stereotypes are common/expected responses to control-display relationships based on previous experience (e.g., turning a dial clockwise to increase volume). <marks>1</marks>

**Advantages of Population Stereotypes**:
- They make products more intuitive, reducing the cognitive load and learning time for the user. <marks>1</marks>
- Their use is critical in safety-critical designs (e.g., medical or automotive) to minimize user error, especially under stress. <marks>1</marks>
- They allow for faster reaction times in human information processing. <marks>1</marks>

**Limitations of Population Stereotypes**:
- Stereotypes are not universal and often vary across different cultures or geographic regions (e.g., light switch directions). <marks>1</marks>
- If a designer ignores these stereotypes, it can lead to user frustration, misuse, or dangerous accidents. <marks>1</marks>

**Use of Demographics**:
- Demographics provide quantitative data (e.g., age, gender, income) that help define and segment the target market. <marks>1</marks>
- They allow designers to select the correct anthropometric data (e.g., the 5th to 95th percentile for a specific age group). <marks>1</marks>
- They help in tailoring product features and pricing to the economic and physical needs of a specific population segment. <marks>1</marks>

**Limitations of Demographics**:
- Demographic data is purely quantitative and does not explain the qualitative 'why' behind user behaviors or preferences. <marks>1</marks>
- Over-reliance on 'average' demographic data can lead to designs that fail to accommodate users at the extremes of the population. <marks>1</marks>

**Synthesis/Conclusio**n:
- Effective product development balances demographic data (identifying *who* the user is) with population stereotypes (identifying *how* they intuitively interact). <marks>1</marks>

Award [1] per valid point or critique with development, up to [9 max].

questions.specification: Why are multidisciplinary teams crucial in UCD?

options.content: They bring diverse perspectives to improve user understanding
They eliminate the need for iterative testing

questions.specification: Which option best illustrates the purpose of a persona in UCD?

options.content: A symbolic logo used in branding
A detailed fictional user representing target demographics
A contract signed by the project sponsor

questions.specification: A healthcare startup is developing an emergency alert wearable device for elderly users living independently. To better understand user needs and environmental limitations, the design team applies a variety of user-centred design (UCD) techniques across the project stages.

parts.content:
The company conducts field testing of a prototype device with elderly users in their homes. See **Figure 1**.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/529a17d6-c381-42d0-9468-92e937510490)

Identify two challenges elderly users may face when activating the wearable device shown in **Figure 1**.
parts.markscheme:
Difficulty pressing small buttons due to reduced dexterity <marks>1 mark</marks>

Inability to read digital display or icons clearly <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for each relevant challenge up to [2 max].*

parts.content: Explain how observing elderly users during real-time emergency simulations can help improve the usability of the product.
parts.markscheme:
Reveals moments where users hesitate or struggle <marks>1 mark</marks>

Captures timing and clarity of system response <marks>1 mark</marks>

Enables testing under real stress conditions to observe user behaviour <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for each developed point up to [3 max].*

parts.content:
To involve end-users in the creative process, the company runs a co-design session with elderly volunteers. See **Figure 2**.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/0a403558-0b37-4239-ace8-5b9218e5136c)

Describe one advantage and one limitation of using co-design workshops with elderly participants.
parts.markscheme:
Advantage: Gives designers authentic insights into daily needs <marks>1 mark</marks>

Limitation: Some elderly participants may struggle to express abstract design ideas <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for each clear advantage/limitation up to [2 max].*

parts.content: Explain how involving elderly users in co-design can influence decisions about interaction and interface features.
parts.markscheme:
Users may request larger icons or simplified menus <marks>1 mark</marks>

Feedback may prompt inclusion of voice prompts or haptic feedback <marks>1 mark</marks>

Involving users can increase product adoption and satisfaction <marks>1 mark</marks>

Designers can prioritise features users find genuinely important <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per well-explained design influence up to [4 max].*

parts.content: Discuss the impact of interdisciplinary collaboration in designing for users with accessibility needs, referencing roles such as occupational therapists, UX designers and engineers.
parts.markscheme:
Occupational therapists advise on physical and cognitive needs <marks>1 mark</marks>

UX designers ensure interface usability for impaired users <marks>1 mark</marks>

Engineers ensure technical feasibility of suggestions <marks>1 mark</marks>

Collaboration reduces the risk of single-discipline bias <marks>1 mark</marks>

Helps balance empathy, usability, and functionality <marks>1 mark</marks>

Improves accessibility compliance and long-term user success <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per clear, discipline-linked point up to [6 max].*

parts.content:
The design team creates an empathy map based on interviews and observation of elderly users. See **Figure 3**.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/e5ea0103-8ef0-4d57-8847-a775c22bc0c0)

Using examples, evaluate the use of empathy maps in reducing design bias and improving product inclusivity.
parts.markscheme:
**Positives**:

Reduces assumptions by grounding decisions in real emotion <marks>1 mark</marks>

Highlights differences across users that might be overlooked <marks>1 mark</marks>

Supports development of inclusive and respectful solutions <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Challenges**:

Designers might over-rely on limited interview data <marks>1 mark</marks>

Empathy maps can oversimplify complex emotional responses <marks>1 mark</marks>

Needs constant updating to stay relevant and unbiased <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per well-developed example up to [6 max], ensuring both sides are addressed.*

questions.specification: A city council is redesigning a public library to better meet the needs of its diverse users, including students, elderly visitors, people with disabilities, and young children. A design consultancy applies a user-centred research approach to gather feedback and iteratively improve the space.

parts.content:
A designer observes how different users interact with a digital kiosk. See **Figure 1**.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/990ea778-2da4-4d48-a151-e5cc41d67b7c)

Identify two specific usability problems an elderly or disabled user might encounter when using the self-checkout kiosk.
parts.markscheme:
- Small on-screen buttons hard to tap with low dexterity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Poor screen contrast making text unreadable for visually impaired users <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how insights gained from observing users like those in Figure 1 can be used to inform inclusive design improvements.
parts.markscheme:
- Designers can identify accessibility issues in real-time, such as unclear instructions or physical height barriers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Results can guide updates such as voice assistance or simplified UI <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Observation helps ensure kiosks meet the needs of less tech-savvy users <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Designers can redesign placement/height based on observed difficulties <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The design team conducts a focus group to evaluate how accessible and welcoming the library space is for a range of users. See **Figure 2**.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/998ed2e3-4f20-4722-b701-a835cf4aeb22)

State one advantage and one limitation of using a focus group in this scenario.
parts.markscheme:
- Advantage: Rich qualitative feedback from a range of users <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limitation: May not reflect needs of underrepresented groups <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how user-centred research with diverse user types (e.g. parents with strollers, wheelchair users, teenagers) enhances the final design of the public space.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies specific physical access issues (e.g. aisle width for wheelchairs) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Improves signage and navigation for users with cognitive or visual needs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourages multi-use zones (e.g. flexible seating for families and teens) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Results in higher satisfaction and usage across all demographic groups <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Prevents biased design that only serves a narrow user base <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports equitable, inclusive public service provision <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The team develops personas based on research to represent typical library visitors. See **Figure 3**.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/96cf34c5-5882-4c40-a530-e4c17a826386)

Explain how the use of personas contributes to the development of empathetic, inclusive design decisions.
parts.markscheme:
- Personas help the team visualize real users’ needs and frustrations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourages designers to avoid assumptions and stereotype-based design <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Facilitates communication and alignment across the team <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Prioritizes inclusive features like screen readers or alternate seating zones <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Which of the following best reflects the core goal of user-centred design (UCD)?

options.content: Meeting user needs, wants, and limitations throughout the design process
options.markscheme: User-centred design (UCD) is an approach to design that grounds the process in information about the people who will use the product. The core goal is to address the needs, wants, and limitations of end users at every stage of the design cycle.

options.content: Enhancing supply chain efficiency through automation
options.markscheme: This refers to operations management or logistics rather than UCD.

options.content: Ensuring brand consistency across global markets
options.markscheme: This is a goal of brand management and marketing.

options.content: Reducing manufacturing costs through modular design
options.markscheme: This is a goal of Design for Manufacture (DfM) or economic efficiency.

parts.content: State two techniques used in user-centred research.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1</marks> for each valid user-centred research technique identified, up to a maximum of <marks>2</marks>.

Valid techniques include:
- user observation <marks>1</marks>
- interviews <marks>1</marks>
- questionnaires / surveys <marks>1</marks>
- focus groups <marks>1</marks>
- task analysis <marks>1</marks>
- user diaries <marks>1</marks>
- usability testing <marks>1</marks>

parts.content: Outline two advantages and two disadvantages of involving users at all stages of the design process.
parts.markscheme:
- State an advantage, such as improved alignment with user needs and expectations <marks>A1</marks>
- State an advantage, such as encouraged innovation through diverse input <marks>A1</marks>
- State a disadvantage, such as increased project time due to repeated testing and feedback <marks>A1</marks>
- State a disadvantage, such as conflicting user opinions which may complicate decision-making <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award [1] per valid advantage/disadvantage, up to [4 max].</note>

questions.specification: How does rapid prototyping enhance the iterative design process?

options.content: By replacing the need for CAD modeling
By outsourcing design tasks to external firms
By accelerating production of refined, testable physical prototypes
By delaying feedback collection until after final approval

questions.specification:
A researcher is interacting with a user as shown in the image. Which user-centred research method is most likely being illustrated?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/a811db1c-6925-4fb3-9e3f-2c2208b55da8.jpeg)

options.markscheme: Focus groups involve a moderated discussion with a small group of people (typically 6–10) to explore their attitudes and feelings about a product.
Contextual inquiry involves observing and interviewing users in their natural environment (e.g., home or workplace) as they perform real-world tasks.
A user interview is a face-to-face interaction where the researcher asks a single user questions to gain qualitative insights into their needs and experiences.
Questionnaires are sets of written questions used to collect data from a large number of people, often focusing on quantitative data.

questions.specification: What is the most direct outcome of ignoring user limitations during product design?
What distinguishes user-centred research from traditional market research?

options.content: It examines user behaviour and context, not just preferences
It often relies on large-scale surveys to measure stated preferences and attitudes
It primarily evaluates a product’s commercial viability rather than how people use it
It typically segments audiences using demographic profiles and purchasing patterns

questions.specification: Scenarios in user-centred design help to:

options.content: Predict how users interact with products in real contexts
Test material safety at extreme temperatures

questions.specification: A population stereotype might influence UCD by:

options.content: Informing interface elements such as color coding

questions.specification:
What stage of UCD does this most likely show?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXdn0jd3aIInilL3DKR45vYqu-dxf0WH3rzhpov3j2guI3dtBc1fldV2JniymNLa6fb1O5-D8tq1CDPKfcDB9kRekwCgyqJHmm9f0XJKyKJUZd4_hGKBC2YpmUolfd-y6_0m-85b?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)
A product designed without consulting potential users is most likely to:

options.content: Underperform due to unmet user expectations

questions.specification: A UCD team is reviewing early prototypes. What feedback would best reflect user-centred values?

options.content: Does this match our brand colour palette?
Is this intuitive and easy to use for our intended user?
Can we produce more units per hour?

questions.specification: How do scenarios help guide design decisions in a user-centred process?

options.content: By removing the need for interdisciplinary teams
By modeling how a product is used in context over time
By reducing the total number of design iterations

questions.specification: Why is it important to involve users early in the design process?

options.content: To ensure the product addresses real user needs

questions.specification:
## Question
What makes User-Centered Design (UCD) distinct from other design approaches?

options.markscheme: Rapid prototyping is a common feature of many design methodologies, such as Agile or iterative design, and is not the primary factor that distinguishes UCD from others.

options.content: Continuous user involvement throughout the design process
options.markscheme: The defining characteristic of User-Centered Design is the active and continuous involvement of users at every stage of the design cycle to ensure the product meets their specific needs and expectations.
Focusing on aesthetic trends is more closely related to visual design or fashion-led design rather than the functional and user-centric focus of UCD.
UCD prioritizes direct feedback and research from actual users rather than relying solely on marketing department data or internal team assumptions.

questions.specification: In a UCD approach, why is it important to understand both the user’s task and their environment?

options.content: It provides insights into production time and cost
It ensures the product fits real-world usage contexts
It helps identify new suppliers for components
It allows for optimization of emotional response during use

questions.specification: A city transport authority is redesigning its public ticketing system to better serve a diverse population, including tourists, elderly people, non-native speakers, and those with visual impairments. The design team uses multiple user-centred design (UCD) strategies to ensure inclusivity and functionality across contexts.

parts.content:
The design team observes elderly users purchasing train tickets at self-service kiosks. See **Figure 1**.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/bfae77d0-0751-4681-a2e3-06d1118efa7e)

Identify two usability issues the elderly person may face in **Figure 1**.
parts.markscheme:
Small font or low contrast on screen makes text hard to read <marks>1 mark</marks>

Touchscreen buttons are too small or unresponsive for shaky hands <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per clearly inferred and relevant challenge from Figure 1 up to [2 max].*

parts.content: Explain how the design team could use task analysis to improve the ticket machine’s interface.
parts.markscheme:
Record each step the user takes when interacting with the kiosk <marks>1 mark</marks>

Identify where users pause, make mistakes, or need assistance <marks>1 mark</marks>

Use findings to simplify navigation flow (e.g. fewer screens) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Adjust button placement or timing to suit motor control limitations <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per valid use of task analysis for UI improvement up to [4 max].*

parts.content:
The team conducts focus groups with a diverse range of daily commuters. See **Figure 2**.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/e2c9ee44-09a2-46f9-bb29-2e5039e6f2b6)

State one benefit and one drawback of using focus groups to gather user feedback.
parts.markscheme:
Benefit: Provides deep insight through discussion and clarification <marks>1 mark</marks>

Drawback: May not be statistically representative of the whole population <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per clear point up to [2 max].*

parts.content: Explain why it is valuable to include users with accessibility needs in focus group discussions.
parts.markscheme:
Highlights design barriers that able-bodied users may overlook <marks>1 mark</marks>

Leads to better empathy in the team’s design thinking <marks>1 mark</marks>

Ensures compliance with universal design standards <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per reasonable explanation up to [3 max].*

parts.content: Discuss how a multidisciplinary team can use feedback from Figure 2 to create a more inclusive ticketing system.
parts.markscheme:
Industrial designers can adjust screen layout and physical height <marks>1 mark</marks>

UX designers can create clearer, faster input flows <marks>1 mark</marks>

Accessibility consultants ensure compatibility with screen readers <marks>1 mark</marks>

Engineers may introduce tactile or voice-based feedback <marks>1 mark</marks>

Marketers may reframe instructions for tourist clarity <marks>1 mark</marks>

The team can prioritise features based on specific needs (e.g. multilingual support) <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per valid, discipline-specific insight that supports inclusion up to [6 max].*

parts.content:
The team builds personas based on commuter research to guide further design decisions. See **Figure 3**.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/46dfe6b0-aad5-46f9-8f2b-e55e54c93cda)

Explain how using personas during the design process benefits communication within the design team.
parts.markscheme:
Provides a shared reference that unifies team understanding of user needs <marks>1 mark</marks>

Clarifies priorities in feature development (e.g. fast access vs. clarity) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Ensures each team member designs for the same user goals <marks>1 mark</marks>

Improves alignment during decision-making and prototyping stages <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per logical benefit to team communication up to [4 max].*

questions.specification:
The visualized board most likely displays:

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXc8QFoEYqtOXuI1mHxXY1kj3QrEijVOPDxNBEr6fg-zSm6NYNik9inReyQKXxNrsrOZSC9JU4vii8hQYgewldznnUPAdC--yAlmuCFlzzROhoK89bQ77fiID_iTFsYUD-eHzgwd5w?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: A user persona to guide design

What do the following images illustrate?
